coredata.dc:title: Novel dual-template molecular imprinted electrochemical sensor for simultaneous detection of CA and TPH based on peanut twin-like NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs nanospheres: Fabrication, application and DFT theoretical study
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Zhiwei
Du, Xin
Sun, Mengmeng
Zhang, Yan
Li, Yifan
Wang, Xianxiang
Wang, Yanying
Du, Haijun
Yin, Huadong
Rao, Hanbing
coredata.dc:description: Hollow peanut-shaped NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 twinned nano-spherical shell composite materials have interconnected electron channels and excellent electrochemical performance, which prompted the use of this unique spatial structure to fabricate efficient electrochemical sensors. In this work, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) incorporated into magnetic NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 nanoparticle shell (NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was applied to construct a dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) based electrochemistry sensor (NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE) for the simultaneous detection of catechin (CA) and theophylline (TPH). MIP was fabricated by an in-situ electrochemical polymerization strategy based on the theoretical exploration and density functional theory (DFT) computer directional simulation to screen out the optimal functional monomer (L-arginine) and the optimal ratio between the dual template molecules (CA and TPH) and functional monomer. The materials were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, and TGA. Besides, electron binding energy, binding constant, and imprinting factor were investigated. With the optimal conditions, the proposed electrochemical dual detection system showed outstanding analytical performance for the simultaneous sensing of CA and TPH, with an ultralow detection limit (LOD, S/N = 3) of 1.3 nM for CA in 0.01–1 μM (R2 = 0.9956) and 1–50 μM (R2 = 0.9928), as well as a LOD of 20.0 nM for TPH in the linear range of 0.1–100 μM (R2 = 0.9939), respectively. Also, the selectivity and anti-interference performances of the fabricated sensor were performed by differential pulse voltammetry and chronoamperometry, and successfully detected the analyte from tea drinks and human urine samples with the recovery rates ranging from 98.22% to 104.76% and relative standard deviations (RSD) were 1.19%–3.81%, demonstrated the sensor has excellent stability, repeatability, and reproducibility, which paves the way for other platforms to use this nanomaterial for the detection of antioxidant in the filed food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual template
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Density functional theory
Catechin
Theophylline
Electrochemical sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Computer simulation of template and monomer in MIPs
Screening of functional monomers and selection of molar ratio between template and monomer
Calculation of binding constant and imprinted factor
Practical assay of the MIP sensor in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support of this work by the Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 2021YJ0316 , 2021YFG0250 , and 2021YJ0343 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21905186 ), and Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 2021993036 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 (A), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs (B–C); TEM image of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 (D) and HRTEM image of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 (E). SEM images of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs (F), and the corresponding elemental mapping (G).

(A) CVs and (C) EIS of different types of modified electrodes in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution contains 0.1 M KCI for bare GCE (a), NCDs/GCE (b), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/GCE (c), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/GCE (d), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE after polymerization (e), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE after elution (f), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE after incubation in 100 μM CA and 100 μM TPH (g). (B) DPVs of bare GCE (a), NCDs/GCE (b), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/GCE (c), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/GCE (d), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE after polymerization and NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/NIP/GCE (f) in PBS solution with the presence of 20 μM CA and 40 μM TPH. (D) EIS of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE after incubation in 100 μM CA and 100 μM TPH (g) with a-f are the same as (C).

(A) DPV responses of CA (0.01–50 μM) and TPH (0.1–100 μM), respectively, and (B–C) corresponding calibration curve on NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE. (D) DPV curves of CA (0.1–50 μM) and TPH (0.5–100 μM), respectively on NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/NIP/GCE, and the relationship between peak currents and concentration in illustration. (E) DPV curves of CA (0.01–75 μM) in exist of 20 μM TPH (inset: the calibration plot of CA). (F) DPV curves of TPH (0.50–200 μM) in exist of 20 μM CA (inset: the calibration plot of TPH).

(A) DPV curves of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE in PBS with 20 μM CA and 40 μM TPH (a) mixed solution, concluding 200 μM MgSO4 (b), CaCl2 (c), KCI (d), NaCl (e), ascorbic acid (f), xanthine (g), caffeine (h), theobromine (i), citric acid (j) and glucose (k), respectively. (B) Corresponding the DPV current responses of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE. (C–D) Interference response of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE in PBS containing CA (20 μM) and TPH (40 μM). (E) DPV responses of imprinted or non-imprinted sensors for CA (20 μM) and 200 μM of structural analogs (Quercetin (QU), Rutin (RU), Morin hydrate (MH)). (F) DPV responses of the imprinted or non-imprinted sensors for TPH (30 μM) and 300 μM of structural analogs (Theobromine (TH), Xanthine (XA), Caffeine (CF)).
Preparation process of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE nanocomposite sensor and simultaneous electrochemical sensing of CA and TPH.
Comparison of this work with various reported electroanalytical works for CA and TPH detection.

head.ce:title: Novel dual-template molecular imprinted electrochemical sensor for simultaneous detection of CA and TPH based on peanut twin-like NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs nanospheres: Fabrication, application and DFT theoretical study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Xin Kang Road, Yucheng District, Ya'an, 625014, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Farm Animal Genetic Resources Exploration and Innovation Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Farm Animal Genetic Resources Exploration and Innovation Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu, Sichuan 611130, P.R. China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zhiwei Lu and Xin Du are joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hollow peanut-shaped NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 twinned nano-spherical shell composite materials have interconnected electron channels and excellent electrochemical performance, which prompted the use of this unique spatial structure to fabricate efficient electrochemical sensors. In this work, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) incorporated into magnetic NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 nanoparticle shell (NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs) modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was applied to construct a dual-template molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) based electrochemistry sensor (NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE) for the simultaneous detection of catechin (CA) and theophylline (TPH). MIP was fabricated by an in-situ electrochemical polymerization strategy based on the theoretical exploration and density functional theory (DFT) computer directional simulation to screen out the optimal functional monomer (L-arginine) and the optimal ratio between the dual template molecules (CA and TPH) and functional monomer. The materials were characterized by SEM, TEM, XRD, XPS, and TGA. Besides, electron binding energy, binding constant, and imprinting factor were investigated. With the optimal conditions, the proposed electrochemical dual detection system showed outstanding analytical performance for the simultaneous sensing of CA and TPH, with an ultralow detection limit (LOD, S/N = 3) of 1.3 nM for CA in 0.01–1 μM (R2 = 0.9956) and 1–50 μM (R2 = 0.9928), as well as a LOD of 20.0 nM for TPH in the linear range of 0.1–100 μM (R2 = 0.9939), respectively. Also, the selectivity and anti-interference performances of the fabricated sensor were performed by differential pulse voltammetry and chronoamperometry, and successfully detected the analyte from tea drinks and human urine samples with the recovery rates ranging from 98.22% to 104.76% and relative standard deviations (RSD) were 1.19%–3.81%, demonstrated the sensor has excellent stability, repeatability, and reproducibility, which paves the way for other platforms to use this nanomaterial for the detection of antioxidant in the filed food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: Drinking tea has always been a fashion and trend. The ingredients in tea include tea polyphenols, tea polysaccharides, protein and amino acids, alkaloids, mineral elements, etc. Among them, catechin (CA, Fig. S1A), as a reducing polyphenol in tea, is a natural lipid antioxidant (Gogoi et al., 2009). Its antioxidant activity even higher than vitamin E, which can scavange free radicals produced by the human body to protect cell membranes (Oliver et al., 2017). Related studies have shown that CA can also inhibit high blood pressure (Wang et al., 2010) and hyperglycemia (Matsumoto et al., 1993), increase the amount of high-density lipoprotein, as well as protects against radiation, and ultraviolet rays (Iacopini et al., 2008). The study found that after volunteers drink tea, the catechin content in the urine excreted within 24 h was 28.5% of the intake (Stalmach et al., 2009). Taking a catechin product with a total dose of 240 mg, the plasma concentration can reach 0.5 μM (Naparlo et al., 2020). Although CAs are extracted from natural products, their harm is obvious once they are ingested in large amounts. For example, applying it directly to the skin may cause redness, swelling, and itching. At the same time, it also has a certain stimulating effect on the respiratory system. Theophylline (TPH, Fig. S1B) is a methylxanthine drug, which possesses the functions of cardiotonic, diuretic, dilating coronary artery, and relaxing bronchial smooth muscle (Kim et al., 2016). It's often used as a bronchodilator to treat asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (Zhao et al., 2020). TPH can relax the bronchial smooth muscle of patients, achieve the effect of antispasmodic and antiasthmatic (Feng et al., 2018), thereby alleviating the spasm of patients. The most suitable plasma TPH concentration for adults is between 5 and 20 μg/mL. When the concentration exceeds 20 μg/mL, it will produce toxicity (Duan et al., 2021). In clinical practice, patients with the intake of a large dosage of TPH may experience adverse reactions such as tachycardia and muscle tremor (Wang et al., 2018b). Ingestion of large doses of CA and THP is considered to be one of the more important ways for human toxicity. Therefore, the intake of CA and TPH should be monitored quantitatively to ensure safety.

Continuous monitoring of CA and TPH in a water environment required highly complex and time-consuming procedures based on the benchtop and expensive instruments. For example, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Li et al., 2020), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Kanazawa et al., 2000), and ultraviolet spectrophotometry (UV) (Ahmadi et al., 2018) usually require sample pretreatment. Therefore, there is a need to develop an alternative method for fast and sensitive analysis. Convenient and low-cost analytical methods have become a hot research trend in present (Zhang et al., 2019).

The emerging molecularly imprinted technology (MIT) based on electrochemical molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) sensors possess the advantages of rapid, sensitive, specific, efficient, and low-cost (Chen et al., 2016), which provides a new idea for the detection of CA, TPH, and other compounds, as well as antibiotics. MIT, also known as artificial antigen-antibody technology (Zhang and Liu, 2019), has attracted much attention in recent years (Jia et al., 2018). MIPs are synthetic biomimetic materials that mimic biological receptors, and the basic principle is to form the highly cross-linked macromolecular structures by polymerization of template molecules and functional monomers. Then, under the full action of eluent, the polymer removes the template molecules and forms an imprinted cavity with special strength and rigidity (Sunayama et al., 2018). The imprinted cavity possessed binding sites towards the template molecules through the intermolecular force and then achieves the purpose of specific recognition (BelBruno, 2019).

In traditional MIT, the selection of the appropriate monomer bound to the assay and the corresponding molar ratio has a great influence on the number and strength of molecularly imprinted cavities (Gajda et al., 2020). The rational design of dual-template MIPs is critical (Lu et al., 2020a). It is necessary to carry out theoretical simulation before the experiment, which can reduce the complexity of the experimental screening process, point out the direction for the next step, and reduce the burden. Use Density functional theory (DFT) to screen monomers and the corresponding molar ratio (Li et al., 2018). Traditional DFT calculations are only for single-template molecular imprinting detection as far as we know (Sun et al., 2021), we are the first to create a DFT calculation with double templates, and its complexity is several times that of a single template. To evaluate the spatial position of easy bonding between template and molecule, the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) of the polymer under the optimal template and ratios were calculated according to the calculated results (Borovina et al., 2019). Compared with precipitation polymerization, electrochemical polymerization based on cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to directly polymerize an imprinted film with high stability on the electrode surface, which has the advantages of rapid and easy preparation. It is known that electrode modification materials play a decisive role in electrochemical sensors (Wang et al., 2018a). Besides, active specific surface area of the imprinted film has a great influence on the analysis results, therefore, the selection of appropriate conductive nanomaterials can improve the performance of electrode devices.

Common precious metal nanoparticles gold, silver and platinum were widely used in electrode modification materials to strengthen the stability and electrical conductivity of sensors (Liu et al., 2019). Because of their extremely high electron movement rate and excellent catalytic property, these metal-based nanomaterials have always been a concern by academia. Controlling the morphology of nanoparticles (NPs) is a good choice to achieve their high sensitivity because of the exposure of active sites. However, the easy agglomeration of these small-sized NPs is a huge problem that researchers have to face in the modification process. Therefore, researchers try to obtain nanomaterials with a large specific surface area through the regulation of surface morphology to make up for the disadvantages brought by the agglomeration of nanoparticles (Ling et al., 2020). Undoubtedly, it is still a huge challenge to uniformly and firmly disperse nanomaterials with excellent morphology and small diameter on the electrode surface.

Due to its unique three-dimensional space structure, hollow sphere nanocomposites have been widely used in the fields of catalysis, nano-reactor, and drug delivery (Wang et al., 2016). In recent years, they have been widely concerned in scientific photonic devices, chemical sensors, biotechnology, energy conversion, and storage systems (Lu et al., 2012). In addition, Fe2+ in Fe3O4 can be partially or completely replaced by other divalent transition ions (such as Ni2+ and Co2+), and has excellent electro-catalytic property (Lima et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2020a). Mahala et al. reported that the nanoplates of CoFe2O4 and nanosheets of NiFe2O4 were successfully fabricated based on a wet-chemical route and calcination process (Mahala et al., 2018). Our group reported that MOF-derived Co3O4/FeCo2O4 nanocomposite was successfully prepared for the sensing of active molecules based on a classic hydrothermal reaction process (Lu et al., 2020b).

N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) are a kind of zero-dimensional nanomaterials that are easy to obtain, low cost, and high biocompatibility (Liao et al., 2018). In addition, they also exhibit large specific surface areas, excellent electron transfer capabilities and strong electronic storage capabilities, all of which give them broad application prospects in the fields of sensors, luminescent materials, and light/electrocatalysts (Lu et al., 2019). NCDs can be designed by many types of functional groups such as rich in multiple hydroxyl groups, nitrogen-oxygen groups, and amino groups (Ghadari et al., 2020), which can adjust their physical and chemical properties. In this paper, the introduction of N-CDS increases the dispersion of the material, promote the conductivity of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4, increases the electrochemically active area, and improves the conductivity stability of the sensor (Tian et al., 2020). NCDs are closely combined with nanocomposites after ultrasonic treatment (Pathak et al., 2019), which can introduce the intermolecular force into the material surface and surrounding areas (Wei et al., 2016). Given these attractive properties, NCDs are expected to be a regulator of the morphology of nanomaterials. At the same time, the doped of NCDs can make CoFe2O4 with an amorphous structure and larger microspheres composed of thicker wafers. Therefore, the electrochemical performance and sensitivity of the sensor will be greatly improved by giving full play to the electrical conductivity of the material (Bali Prasad et al., 2017).

Regarding the quantitative analysis of CA and TPH, predecessors have studied a lot. Jian et al. used the N-doped carbon-modified electrode to detect CA (Pang et al., 2017). Duran et al. used triaminoimide modified electrodes to detect CA in green tea (Duran et al., 2020). Feng et al. developed a nanopore thin film-based sensor to detect TPH in plant extracts and serum (Feng et al., 2018), and Wang et al. used Hand -in-hand RNA nanowire-based aptasensor realizes the analysis of TPH in biological agents (Wang et al., 2018b). CA and TPH exist at the same time in tea products and are only beneficial to the human body within a certain concentration range. However, no one has ever proposed a scheme for simultaneous high-efficiency and high-sensitivity detection of CA and TPH. Therefore, this article combines DFT theory to guide a bifunctional molecular imprinting system.

Considering the adsorption characteristics and well redox activity of iron and cobalt and nickel-based oxide nanomaterials, in this work, we introduce a simple strategy employing NCDs as dopants to twin-like of magnetic NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs for selective recognition of CA and TPH simultaneously. Its unique three-dimensional core-shell structure has a good spatial framework and level, which endows him with excellent electrical conductivity and electrocatalytic activity (Sagu et al., 2017), can effectively increase the effective specific surface area of the electrode and can load a large number of effective imprinting sites for specific amplification the electro-oxidation signal of the test substance. The NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 spherical shell was synthesized by one-step pyrolysis of the Fe–Co–Ni tri-metal precursor at high temperature in a muff furnace. NCDs were fabricated by the direct hydrothermal treat of citric acid and ammonia mixture. NCDs were incorporated into the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 spherical, and through an effective etching strategy, the morphology and structure of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 spherical are regulated to generate hollow peanut twin-like NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs nanospheres. DFT and package Gaussian 16 were used to calculate the binding energy of template molecule and functional monomer complex, molecular electrostatic potential, and the functional monomers were selected from 5 candidate complexe. Dual template synthesis of magnetic-MIP was thus performed with L-Arg as functional monomers, CA and THP as template molecules, as well as NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs nanospheres as electrode material based on electrochemical polymerization under the guidance of theoretical calculation results (Scheme 1 ). Benefiting from the advanced structure and high electrical conductivity of nanospheres, magnetic-MIP shows excellent electron transfer characteristics and excellent structural stability. The affinity of magnetic-MIP towards CA and THP exhibited an outstanding preconcentration capacity. The morphologies, structures, and electrochemical performance of the synthesized NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE sensor were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), respectively. Then, binding constant, imprinting factor, and anti-interference were also investigated. Finally, the performance of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE for sensing of CA and TPH was evaluated in actual tea water and human urine.

All the experimental parts, including the required reagents and instruments, the actual process of molecular imprinting preparation and the electrochemical inspection were presented in supporting material.

All quantum chemical calculations were performed using Gaussian 16 C.01 program. The spin-unrestricted LC-WPBE functional with 6–31G (d, p) basis set was employed to perform the conformation optimizations. Vibration frequency calculations were performed to characterize the properties of each stagnation point and zero-point energy (ZPE) correction was performed. A tight convergence (10−12 au) criterion was employed, and the solvent water was considered using the IEF-PCM solvent model.

To identify the position of adsorption and further calculate the binding energy of catechins and theophylline with different functional monomers and different ratios under the same template, a natural bond orbital (NBO) calculation was performed (Weinhold et al., 2016). It is shown that the O and N atoms have a slightly higher negative charge distribution, respectively. Therefore, we calculated the adsorption energy (Ead) between theophylline, catechin, L-arginine, o-aminophenol, o-phenylenediamine, salicylic acid, and acrylamide were calculated according to Eq. (1):
(1)
Δ E =
E C
−
E
o r g
where ΔE is the binding energy difference, E
C is the energy of complex, and E
org is the sum of the energies of the functional monomer. Furthermore, the ratios of theophylline, catechin, and l-arginine at 1:2:5, 1:3:5, 2:1:5 were also calculated, respectively, to obtain the optimization ratio.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computer simulation of template and monomer in MIPs
Screening of functional monomers and selection of molar ratio between template and monomer
ce:section.ce:para: In MIPs, different analytes correspond to one or more functional monomers, which have a great influence on the stability, binding energy, and hydrogen bond number of the MIPs. In addition, the appropriate amount of functional monomers and the ratio between monomer and template molecules are also crucial for the strength of the imprinted membrane and the number of imprinted cavities. In this work, different functional monomers were screened through computer targeted simulation and DFT theoretical calculation as shown in Fig. S2. The addition of the L-arginine, o-aminophenol, o-phenylenediamine, salicylic acid, and acrylamide reduced the energies around CA and TPH molecules by at least about 29 kcal mol−1. Herein, when the ratio of TPH, CA, and L-arginine was 1:1:5, the binding energy had the minimum value of 50.1 kcal mol−1, thus the complex was the most stable. Furthermore, the binding energy of TPH, CA and l-arginine at 1:2:5, 1:3:5, 2:1:5 were also calculated ( Fig. S3 and Fig. S4). These calculation results suggested that the ratio of 1:2:5 has the lowest binding energy and the most corresponding hydrogen bonds (Table S1). Besides, other more detailed data and evidence were provided in the supporting material.

To identify the position of adsorption, 1CA-2TPH-5L-Arg was taken as the research object for NBO calculation (Shahabi and Tavakol, 2017). Fig. 1 shows the O and N atoms have a slightly higher negative charge distribution, while the H atom possesses a slightly higher positive charge distribution in all organic molecules, respectively. Therefore, we can infer that the active sites are mainly concentrated in the oxygen atom of carbonyl groups, the hydrogen atom of amine groups, the hydrogen atom, and the oxygen atom of hydroxyl groups (Hu et al., 2020).

Inspired by outstanding catalytic performances of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs as well as the unique adsorption ability of magnetic MIPs (mMIPs) toward template molecule (CA and TPH), we proposed the sensing mechanism for simultaneous assay of CA and TPH on the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE based on adsorption-assisted in situ electrocatalysis. Based on excellent adsorption ability, and the excellent catalytic properties of the magnetic NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs (Fig. S5), CA and TPH were first adsorbed on the mMIPs’ surface, following CA and TPH easily diffused from the outer layer surface of mMIPs to the surface of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs. Subsequently, the electrochemical detection of CA and TPH was performed by preconcentrating the CA and TPH on NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP and a further readout on modified GCE by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV).

Firstly, the synthesizing materials’ morphologies were tasted by SEM and TEM. Both NiFe2O4 and CoFe2O4 are spherical in Fig. S6A and Fig. S6B. Only by adding three metal salts of iron, cobalt, and nickel to replace two of them at the same synthesis process with a mild one-step solvothermal way employing cyclodextrin as the template, followed by calcination, the fabricated NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 composites exhibited an obvious spherical structure (Fig. 2 A). Two key strategies are involves in the fabrication of the core-shell hollow structures of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 products, e.g, nucleation of carbon source (cyclodextrin) and adsorption of Ni2+, Fe3+ and Co2+ on oxygen-containing functional groups, i.e., –OH and –COOH and repeated separation of the oxide shell and inner part during annealing (Wang et al., 2016). When NCDs were incorporated into NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 nanosphere, the obtained NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs composite materials show peanut twin-like nanoshell structure (Fig. 2B). Compared with Fig. 2A, the surface of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 nanospheres modified by NCDs is smoother, more concentrated, and the effective surface area is increased, thus improving the effect of the prepared high-performance imprinted polymer film. Upon the high magnification of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs surface, that the top of the twin-like composite material was etched with a hole by the functionalized of NCDs can be observed (Fig. 2C). The negative of NCDs could attract the positive metal cations, the feed mass and the type of surface charge of NCDs were found to be regulated the morphology then control the growth of crystal (Liu et al., 2020). Besides, the size distribution and surface states of NCDs are further studied in supporting material (Fig. S7). This hollow special structure of layer by layer assembly can provide high specific surface area and accelerate electron transfer. Besides, TEM image of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 composites again demonstrated that the successful preparation of nanospheres (Fig. 2D). The high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) in Fig. 2E has some lattice fringes with the distance of 0.295 nm, 0.264 nm, and 0.447 nm, corresponding to the (220), (311), and (111) lattice plane, that consistent with previous literature reports (Zhang et al., 2017). It is further confirmed that the configuration and crystal structure of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 have been synthesized. Moreover, the EDS results of elemental mapping demonstrated that the C, N, O, Ni, Co, and Fe are uniformly distributed in the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs composites (Fig. 2F-G). Furthermore, Fig. S6(C-D) displays the SEM images of MIP and NIP comparison. We noticed that the pore structure of MIP (Fig. S6C) was different from that of NIP (Fig. S6D), which may be related to the cavity after elution of template molecules. Moreover, the irregular spherical nanoparticles with high cross-linking between them can be seen in Fig. S6C and Fig. S6D, also confirmed the successful preparation of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP.

In addition, EDS spectra of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs (Fig. S8A), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE elution before (Fig. S8B) and after elution (Fig. S8C) were analyzed. Compared with NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs, a molecularly imprinted film layer with uniform distribution and sealing is formed on the surface of the material after electropolymerization (Fig. S8B), and NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs composites were covered by the imprinted film, thus the percentage of metal elements could not be analyzed. After elution, a large number of imprinted cavities are formed, and the nanospheres are exposed so that the percentage content of metal elements can be detected (Fig. S8C). This indicates that the imprinted membrane was successfully synthesized and eluted on the surface of the material. Besides, the composition, valence and thermodynamic properties of the materials were characterized by XRD, TGA, and XPS (Fig. S9 and Fig. S10). The results and detailed discussion were shown in the supporting material. The above analysis results indicated the successful synthesis of the peanut twin-like NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs nanospheres and the MIPs.

To further confirm the electrochemical performance of different modified electrodes, CV, DPV, and EIS were used to characterize. CV characterization was performed from −0.2 V to 0.6 V with a scan rate of 50 mV/s in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. As presented in Fig. 3 A, when GCE was modified by a single NCDs or NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4, the response current of redox peak increased significantly compared with bare GCE, which was ascribed to the excellent conductivity of NCDs and NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4. The redox peak currents of the GCE surface co-modified by NCDs and NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 increased significantly, and the current increase was higher much than that of the single NCDs or NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 modified electrode. When a dense MIP or NIP film is polymerized on the surface of the composite by CV polymerization, the electron transfer rate was blocked, resulting in a significant decrease in the electrical conductivity of the modified electrode. After the template molecules were eluted from the MIP membrane, a part of imprinted cavities was formed, which makes [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- probe easily pass through these cavities to reach the material surface. Therefore, the electrical conductivity of the eluted molecularly imprinted polymer will be increased.

DPV test was performed in PBS containing 20 μM CA and 40 μM TPH. According to Fig. 3B, that the peak position of CA located at 0.23 V while TPH at 1.04 V. However, a significant difference was found in the response currents among NCDs (a), NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 (b), and NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs (c) modified GCE, respectively, which was consistent with the conductivity of the materials in CV diagram. These results fully prove that the higher the conductivity of the material, the faster the oxidation process of CA and TPH, the higher the peak value of response current will be. For dual template MIP, some cavities complementary to the structure of CA and TPH were obtained after eluted template molecules. Due to the imprinted rigid structure, these cavities can only specifically recognize the template molecules, so during the re-adsorption process, the template molecules are rapidly enriched into the imprinted cavity, resulting in the amplification of the current response (curve e). However, NIP can't combine with CA and TPH specifically because there is no template added in the synthesis process, which makes it impossible to form an imprinted cavity in the elution process. Therefore, the detection effect of NIP is the same as that of GCE. Unlike MIP, NIP does not specifically identify cavities, and its current response to CA and TPH were reduced.

EIS was carried out at 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, as shown in Fig. 3C. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) is expressed as the semicircle diameter of the Nyquist diagram. The diameter order of GCE modified by different nanomaterials was NCDs > NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 > NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the synergistic enhancement of NCDs and NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4 composites. The semicircle diameter increased after polymerization of a layer of dense film. This blocking effect was based on the characteristics of the un-eluting MIP film insulation. Once eluted, the departure of the template molecules creates imprinted holes in the MIP. However, its electron transfer impedance will be smaller relative to the un-eluting MIP. Notably, the imprinted impedance is higher than that of bare GCE due to the addition of functional monomers in the polymerization process. Thus, this result coincides with CV. After incubated for a period of time in 100 μM CA and 200 μM TPH, the imprinted cavity recombined with a large number of template molecules resulting in the high impedance, which may be due to the non-conductivity of the template molecules. Besides, these results also indicated that the excellent adsorption performance of the imprinted cavity.

According to the relationships between the peak current and the square root (ν1/2) of different scan rates (Fig. S11, and Fig. S13), it can be fully deduced that the electrochemical system follows a typical diffusion-controlled mechanism based on the Randles-Sevcik equation (Bali Prasad et al., 2017). The active surface area of the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE was 0.275 cm2. The electron transfer coefficients of CA and TPH were calculated to be 0.5024 and 0.4463, respectively. Besides, the electron transfer numbers of both CA and TPH were calculated to be 2, which is the same as the results reported in the article. The reaction mechanism of CA and TPH was shown in Fig. S12. When calculating the surface concentration (r0) and the diffusion coefficient D, the Cottrell equation is used. The r0 values of CA and TPH are 3.06 × 10−10 mol cm−2 and 1.17 × 10−10 mol cm−2, and the D value is 1.248 × 10−2 cm−2 s−1and 3.620 × 10−4 cm−2 s−1. In addition, the Velasco equation was used to calculate the standard rate constant (K). The K values of CA and TPH were calculated to be 1.9 × 10−1 cm s−1 and 0.42 × 10−1 cm s−1, respectively. The calculation and detailed description of the electrochemical parameters related to the system were all in the Supporting material.
The optimization of the electropolymerization cycle, scan rate, molar ratio, pH, elution time, and incubation time can be seen from the Supporting material.

The linear ranges and sensitivity of this fabricated sensor (NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE) for simultaneous assay of CA and TPH were investigated by DPV under the optimized experimental conditions. Both enhanced current response signals were obtained accompanied by increased concentrations of CA and TPH, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4 A, electrochemical oxidation peaks of CA and TPH on our sensor were located at 0.23 V and 1.04 V, respectively. Besides, a significant CA secondary oxidation peak can be observed at around 0.6 V, which may be attributed to the secondary electron transfer during CA oxidation process, and is consistent with literature reports (El-Hady, 2007). The linear relationship between currents response and CA concentrations were achieved in a range from 0.01 to 50.0 μM. Two linear regression equations of Ip (μA) = 1.5833·C
CA (μM) + 0.1713 (R2 = 0.9956, 0.01–1.0 μM), and Ip (μA) = 0.7519·C
CA (μM) + 1.4187 (R2 = 0.9928, 1.0–50.0 μM) for CA were calculated. However, a linear regression equation of Ip (μA) = 0.2098·C
TPH (μM) + 0.5043 (R2 = 0.9939, 0.1–100 μM) was achieved for TPH. Ultra-low limits of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) were calculated to be 1.3 nM for CA and 20 nM for TPH based on 3σ value of blank. At the same time, the limit of quantification (LOQ = 10σ/S) were calculated to be 4.3 nM for CA and 67 nM for TPH based on 10σ value of blank. This dual-template MIP electrochemical sensor possessed excellent performance, e.g., low detection limit, short detection time, and wide linear range compared to the previously reported method, which is much better than the existing nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensors (Table 1 ). In addition, the performance of the NIP sensor (NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/NIP/GCE) was also investigated for simultaneous detection of CA (0.1–50.0 μM) and TPH (0.5–100.0 μM) for comparison purposes, as shown in Fig. 4D. We can see that there is no linear relationship between the peak current and the concentration of CA and TPH. Besides, the response currents of CA and TPH in the NIP sensor were lower than the MIP sensor. This may be attributed to the fact that the imprinted holes were not formed and could not specifically recognize the template molecules.

Moreover, the mutual interference between CA and TPH on the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE was assessed. As shown in Fig. 4E, the concentration of TPH was fixed at 20 μM, and the concentration of CA increased from 10 nm to 75 μM. It can be clearly observed that the response current of CA increased, while the response current of TPH remained constant. The curve equations of Ip (μA) = 2.3955·C
CA (μM) + 0.1056 (R2 = 0.9924, 0.01–1.0 μM), inset in Fig. 4E, and Ip (μA) = 0.8358·C
CA (μM) + 1.8901 (R2 = 0.9986, 1.0–75.0 μM) were obtained, respectively (Fig. 4E). Similar results can be observed in Fig. 4F by fixing the concentration of CA at 20 μM and increasing the concentration of TPH from 0.5 μM to 200 μM, and a regression equation of Ip (μA) = 0.1987·C
TPH (μM) + 1.1248 (R2 = 0.9961). The sensitivity of detection CA and TPH separately was similar to that of the simultaneous assay (2.51 vs 1.72. for CA and 4.70 vs 5.11 TPH), suggesting that there was no interference between CA and TPH that occurred for this sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of binding constant and imprinted factor
ce:section.ce:para: The specific binding constants (K) of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE sensor to CA (Kd1) and TPH (Kd3) were 1.48 μmol L−1 and 2.31 μmol L−1, as shown in Fig. S14(A–B), respectively. For comparison, the binding constants of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/NIP/GCE sensor to CA (Kd2) and TPH (Kd4) were 6.81 μmol L−1 and 8.62 μmol L−1, as shown in Fig. S14(C-D), respectively. Obviously, Kd1 was less than Kd3, and Kd2 was less than Kd4, and Kd1 was the smallest of these binding constants. Therefore, both MIP and NIP sensors for CA have better selectivity than TPH, which is consistent with the result that DPV has a greater response to CA than TPH. It is possible that CA is easily oxidized due to its high electrical activity. Besides, Kd1 was less than kd2 and Kd3 was less than kd4, indicating that the selectivity of the MIP sensor was higher than that of the NIP sensor (Gong et al., 2016). Due to the specific recognition sites and adsorption of MIPs, the template molecules are easily absorbed in the imprinted cavity, resulting in higher selectivity of MIPs than NIPs. The binding constant is specified in the supporting material .

Various important parameters, e.g. selectivity and reproducibility of electrochemical sensors must be considered in analytical applications. MIPs have specifically selective for template molecules due to the recognition is based on the rebinding of template molecules with their complementary binding sites. Some important factors affecting the selectivity of the MIP sensor are the coexisting molecules or ions, structural analogs, size, and functional group of the molecules. For simultaneous assay of CA and TPH, potential interferents, such as MgSO4, CaCl2, KCI, NaCl, ascorbic acid, xanthine, caffeine, theobromine, citric acid, and glucose at a high concentration of 0.1 mM (20 fold concentration of CA and 10 fold concentration of TPH were added to investigate the interference of these substances. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the currents response in DPV remains unchanged, indicating that there is no interference of these substances in the simultaneous detection of CA and TPH. In addition, considering that the oxidation potential of CA and TPH is 0.23 V and 1.04 V, respectively, the potential interference of these substances was investigated by chronoamperometry (i-t). As shown in Fig. 5C, three times of continuous addition of the same concentration of CA (20 μM) at 0.23 V applied potential, significant currents response platforms were obtained. Finally, the same concentration of CA has been added continuously again, and three obvious current response platforms appeared again. Similar results can be observed when a voltage of 1.04 V was applied in the selective experiment of TPH. Above results demonstrate that a satisfactory selectivity was obtained on the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE sensor. In addition, the interference studies were conducted in the presence of template analogs with similar structure, e.g. Quercetin (QU), Rutin (RU), and Morin hydrate (MH) for CA, and Theobromine (TH), Xanthine (XA), and Caffeine (CF) for TPH, respectively. Fig. 5E and F shows the current responses of these analogs at the MIP and NIP sensors. Compared with NIP, the dual-template MIP sensor are quantitatively more sensitive to both CA and TPH than other interference molecules. Since the dual template MIP sensor prepared consists of specific binding sites created by CA and TPH, only these two molecules can bind to these cavities more efficiently and thus possesses the highest sensitivity. However, the structures and functional groups of these molecules are similar to those of the template molecules, thus imprinting factor (IF) was used to further evaluate the performance of the NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE sensor. IF was calculated by the following formula, IF = IMIP/INIP, where IMIP and INIP represent the current response of imprinted electrochemical sensors and non-imprinted electrochemical sensors to the current detection substance, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5E, the IF value of CA was calculated to be 8.452 in the PBS containing 20 μM CA, while other substances, such as QU, RU, and MH, have an IF value close to 1. Similarly, the IF value of TPH was calculated to be 4.371 in the PBS containing 40 μM TPH (Fig. 5F), while the IF value of TH, XA, and CF were calculated to be close to 1. Above results show that NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE has excellent specific recognition ability, and the sensor has good selectivity. It may be related to the imprinting cavity recognition site with a certain strength corresponding to a lot of structures formed on the surface.

To evaluate the reproducibility for the electrochemical sensor, 10 batches of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE were prepared with the same conditions. DPV was determined under the optimal conditions in PBS with 20 μM of CA and 40 μM of TPH. The RSD of CA and TPH were as low as 3.2% and 4.2%, respectively, indicating the good reproducibility of the sensor.

In addition, the repeatability of the one-time NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE was also determined severely. In this experiment, under the conditions of CA 20 μM and TPH 40 μM, previously optimized optimal elution and adsorption time were used for continuous elution and re-absorption processes up to 5, and the RSD of DPV corresponding strength of CA and TPH was calculated to be 2.8% and 3.9%, shows that the sensor has good stability.

Similarly, the stability of the sensor was evaluated at CA of 20 μM and TPH of 40 μM. After the prepared NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE were stored at room temperature for four weeks in the refrigerator, the peak current intensity of CA and TPH was 95.5% and 95.7% of the initial current response. Good stability was demonstrated in this evaluation. Excellent reproducibility, repeatability, and stability are beneficial to electrochemical analysis of CA and TPH by electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical assay of the MIP sensor in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Master Kang's green tea drink samples were purchased from the local store of Sichuan Agricultural University, and the urine samples were obtained from the Hospital of Sichuan Agricultural University. In this paper, the standard addition method was used to calculate the recovery of each sample by three parallel determination experiments. As the results depicted in Table S2, the recoveries of the method were obtained in the range of 98.22%–104.76% with the RSD values were obtained in the range of 1.19%–3.26%.

According to Table S3, the recoveries of HPLC results were in the range of 99.23%–101.23% and the RSD values were in the range of 0.35%–1.29%. The above results reconfirmed that the dual functional MIP electrochemical sensor was reliable for the simultaneous determination of catechin and theophylline.

Besides, T test and F test were used to evaluate whether there is a systematic error in the output data of the sensor, T values both below t
0.05,2 = 4.30 were achieved for CA and TPH, indicating no significant difference between the true and measured values at the 95% confidence level (Table S2). The comparable t-values of the actual samples were obtained by HPLC method. Standard deviation (SD) of the proposed voltammetry method was compared with that of HPLC method, and the obtained of F-values (Table S3) were smaller than the critical ones (19.00, n = 3). These indicates that there is no significant difference and/or systematic error between the HPLC method and voltammetry method at the 95% confidence level.

In this work, the dual-template MIPs sensors were prepared for assaying CA and TPA simultaneously by electropolymerization method based on peanut twin-like NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs nanospheres. Computer targeted simulation calculations were applied to investigate the optimal functional monomer, and the optimal ratio between the dual template molecules (CA and TPH) and functional monomer. Therefore, DFT calculation provides valuable guidance for the design of MIP sensors.

The combination of the dual imprinting effect of the MIP sensor and the electrocatalysis of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs improved the sensitivity of NiFe2O4/CoFe2O4/NCDs/MIP/GCE for CA and TPH detection. An excellent performance MIP sensor was prepared for detecting CA in the concentration range of 0.01–1.0 μM with LOD of 0.0013 μM, and 1.0–50.0 μM for the detection of TPH with LOD of 0.02 μM, respectively. The newly designed dual template MIP sensor possessed outstanding reproducibility, repeatability, stability, and high selectivity than NIP. This sensor can be used in the analysis of actual samples and obtain satisfactory results. Thus, the analysis exhibited that the electrochemical sensor provides an efficient, rapid, sensitive, and accurate method for the detection of CA and TPH.

Zhiwei Lu: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Xin Du: Writing – review & editing. Mengmeng Sun: Data curation. Yan Zhang: Conceptualization. Yifan Li: Supervision. Xianxiang Wang: Resources, Supervision. Yanying Wang: Resources, Funding acquisition. Haijun Du: Resources, Funding acquisition. Huadong Yin: Resources, Conceptualization, Investigation. Hanbing Rao: Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support of this work by the Science and Technology Program of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 2021YJ0316, 2021YFG0250, and 2021YJ0343), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21905186), and Two-Way Support Team Programs of Sichuan Agricultural University (Project No. 2021993036).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Sagu et al., 2017
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Sagu, J.S., Wijayantha, K.G.U., Tahir, A.A., 2017. Electrochim Acta 246, 870-878.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Senocak et al., 2019
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Senocak, A., Basova, T., Demirbas, E., Durmus, M., 2019. Electroanalysis 31(9), 1697-1707.
